---
title: A brief introduction to CUDA
author: Marie-Hélène Burle
reference-location: margin
---

:::{.def}

[NVIDIA CUDA Toolkit](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda/toolkit) (usually referred to as "CUDA") is a proprietary set of APIs, tools, and libraries by [NVIDIA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nvidia) that allow to run parallel computing on [graphics processing units (GPUs)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit).

The toolkit includes a C/C++ compiler, debugging, benchmarking, and optimization tools, a [runtime library](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Runtime_library), and many GPU-accelerated libraries.

Many open-source tools build on CUDA to provide GPU-acceleration in languages such as Python or Julia.

:::

## History

The use of GPUs for mathematical calculations rather than image rendering ([general-purpose computing on GPUs or GPGPU)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General-purpose_computing_on_graphics_processing_units)) was a nice and difficult research endeavour involving passing data through graphics shaders.

By providing a standard C++ programming model that removed the need to understand graphics APIs, [CUDA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CUDA) brought GPGPU to the mainstream.

## Support

### Operating systems

| OS      | CUDA support                                                                |
|---------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Linux   | Yes                                                                         |
| Windows | Yes, with limitations, provided Microsoft Visual Studio (MSVS) is installed |
| WSL 2   | Yes, with limitations                                                       |
| macOS   | No[^1]                                                                      |

: {tbl-colwidths="[25, 75]"}

[^1]: Legacy experimental support on older systems was abandoned.

### Accelerators

| Accelerator | CUDA support              |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| NVIDIA GPUs | Yes                       |
| Apple GPUs  | No                        |
| AMD GPUs    | Yes, but not natively[^2] |
| TPUs        | No[^3]                    |

: {tbl-colwidths="[25, 75]"}

[^2]: See below.
[^3]: TPUs use XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra) as their compiler backend.

### Support for AMD GPUs

3 tools with different approaches exist to run CUDA on AMD hardware:

|                   | [HIP](https://github.com/ROCm/hip) | [ZLUDA](https://github.com/vosen/ZLUDA) | [SCALE tookit](https://docs.scale-lang.com/stable/) |
|-------------------|------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| Functioning       | `hipify` CUDA code into neutral language, then compile to target GPU (AMD or NVIDIA) | Translates compiled CUDA code at runtime to execute on AMD GPUs | Compiles CUDA code directly into AMD GPU binaries with SCALE compiler instead of `nvcc` |
| Code modification required | Yes                       | No                                      | No                                                  |
| Recompilation required     | Yes                       | No                                      | Yes                                                 |
| Performance       | Native                             | Near-native                             | Native                                              |
| Maturity          | Yes                                | Yes                                     | No                                                  |
| Library gap       | Yes                                | Yes                                     | Yes                                                 |
| Developer         | AMD (ROCm ecosystem)               | Community driven                        | Spectral Compute (startup)                          |
| Open-source       | Yes                                | Yes                                     | No                                                  |
| **Pros**              | **Native performance, officially supported** | **Very easy to use**                  | **Native performance, easier than HIP** |
| **Cons**              | **Requires a lot of work**             | **Lower performance, might violate NVIDIA’s End User License Agreement** | **Proprietary and less tested** |

: {tbl-colwidths="[10, 30, 30, 30]"}

### On Alliance supercomputers

**The good news is that the Alliance supercomputers use Linux and most GPUs are NVIDIA's (there are a few AMD's on the Nibi cluster).**

CUDA is installed as an [Lmod](https://lmod.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) module on the clusters:

```{.bash}
# To see available versions:
module spider cuda

# To check the needed dependencies of a specific version (e.g. 12.6):
module spider cuda/12.6

# To load a version:
module load cuda/12.6
```

## Alternatives to CUDA

|          |Open| Usage|More info|
|-------------------|------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| **ROCm** |Yes|GPGPU |Part of the [AMD ROCm](https://github.com/ROCm/ROCm) stack for [AMD](https://simple.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_Micro_Devices) GPUs|
|**Triton**|No ([OpenAI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenAI))|Deep learning|Python-based [domain-specific language (DSL)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain-specific_language) and compiler|
|**OpenCL**|Yes|GPGPU|Runs on most hardware, but with lower performance than CUDA and a smaller toolkit|
|**SYCL**|Yes (supported by [Intel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intel))|GPGPU|Runs on most hardware, but with lower performance than CUDA and a smaller toolkit|
|**Vulkan**|Yes|Graphics & GPGPU|Runs on most hardware, but with much lower performance for GPGPU than CUDA|
|**OpenACC**|Yes|Offload loops to GPUs by inserting compiler hints in the code|Runs on most hardware, but restricted to C, C++, and Fortran and lower performance than CUDA. Easy solution for old codebase in compiled languages|

## CUDA for Python

### Low-level wrappers

There are two wrappers that provide low-level control of CUDA in Python:

- [PyCUDA](https://github.com/inducer/pycuda):  an older, community-driven library.
- [CUDA Python](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuda-python): a newer stack by NVIDIA.

For new projects and unless you are already familiar with PyCUDA, favour [CUDA Python](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda/python).

CUDA Python consists of [a collection of packages](https://nvidia.github.io/cuda-python/latest/).

You write CUDA kernels in C++ `.cu` files, compile them using `nvcc`, then execute them using `cuda-python`.

### High-level for NumPy

[CuPy](https://github.com/cupy/cupy) is a high-level drop-in replacement for [NumPy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy) and [SciPy](https://github.com/scipy/scipy). It also allows to pass custom kernels to CUDA.

### High-level for NumPy with JIT

[Numba](https://github.com/numba/numba) is a NumPy optimizing library using the [just-in-time (JIT)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Just-in-time_compilation) compiler [LLVM](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/).

The [numba-cuda](https://github.com/NVIDIA/numba-cuda) package by NVIDIA uses the low-level `cupy-python` bindings from CUDA Python under the hood and allows to run Numba on GPUs.

### High-level for DataFrames

[RAPIDS cuDF](https://github.com/rapidsai/cudf) is a library allowing to run Python DataFrames operations on GPUs. It can be used in 3 ways:

|               | cuDF                       | cuDF pandas module                                        | Polars with GPU engine                       |
|---------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| API           | Similar to pandas<br>Some differences | Exactly the same as pandas                          | Exactly the same as Polars lazy API       |
| Performance   | **Very good** <br>Operations fully run on GPU           | **Good** <br>Automatic fallbacks to CPU for unsupported operations can lead to costly transfers between CPU and GPU  | **The best** <br>Lazy execution <br>Operations  fully run on GPU |

: {tbl-colwidths="[10, 30, 30, 30]"}

### High-level for ML

[RAPIDS cuML](https://github.com/rapidsai/cuml) is a suite of libraries with an API similar to [scikit-learn](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn)'s that allow to run tabular machine learning tasks on GPUs.

### High-level for DL

Many deep learning frameworks come with CUDA integration. This allows to run code on GPU with no (e.g. [JAX](https://github.com/jax-ml/jax)) or little (e.g. [PyTorch](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch), [TensorFlow](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow)) effort.
